preFix.title: Draughtproofing a bathroom extractor fan
preFix.subtitle: Fitting a draught shutter and upgrading to a humidistat-controlled fan
preFix.excerpt: An intermittent extractor fan is one of the most draughty openings in a well-sealed house when not running. A fan with a built-in or add-on draught shutter cuts air ingress to near-zero when the fan is off. This covers the shutter retrofit and the case for a humidistat replacement.
preFix.sourceNotes: Building Regulations Part F (2021) ventilation requirements for bathrooms. Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. BEAMA fan specification guidance.

content.text: A standard 100 mm extractor fan ducted through an external wall or into the roof space creates a 100 mm diameter opening in the building fabric. When the fan is off and the fan blades are stationary, cold air passes through the duct and the fan housing at a rate that can exceed the heat loss through a badly fitted letterbox. An intermittent fan in a well-sealed bathroom is often the largest remaining draughty opening after other draughtproofing work.

Most extractor fans sold in the UK post-2010 include a built-in draught shutter, a plastic or rubber flap that closes by gravity when the fan stops and opens against the airflow when the fan runs. If your fan does not have a shutter, an inline duct shutter can be fitted in the duct run, typically between the fan housing and the external wall. Inline shutters are gravity-close. They need to be accessible for annual inspection as they can stick open with grease or dust build-up.
A humidistat extractor fan runs continuously at a low background speed (typically 5 to 8 litres per second) and boosts to full speed (15 litres per second as required by

) when humidity rises. It drops back to trickle when humidity falls. This is more effective at removing moisture than an on/off fan linked to the light switch, because it runs during overnight humidity-recovery periods after a bath or shower.

The draught issue: a humidistat fan running at 5 litres per second is always extracting a small amount of warmed air, 24 hours a day. This is an intentional background ventilation flow and is the correct trade-off for condensation prevention in a sealed bathroom. The running cost at trickle speed is around 5 to 10 watts continuously. This small energy use is the cost of avoiding condensation in a bathroom with no other ventilation.

Electrical safety. The fan circuit in a bathroom is a controlled zone under Part P (electrical safety). In Zone 1 (above the bath or shower tray to 2.25 m above) and Zone 2 (outside Zone 1, up to 600 mm radius from the shower head), only IP44-rated or higher fans may be installed and wiring must be installed by a registered electrician. The fan housing itself is typically above the shower zone and may be DIY-fitted to an existing switched spur, but wiring changes in the bathroom require a registered electrician or Part P notification.
Isolate the fan circuit at the consumer unit. Test the circuit is dead with a tester before touching wiring.

Remove the fan cover and unscrew the fan body from the wall. Photograph the wiring before disconnecting. A standard bathroom fan connects to Live, Neutral, and Earth; a humidistat fan may have additional wires for the boost relay if boosted separately from the continuous-run circuit.

Fit the new fan body to the same wall aperture (most 100 mm fans share the same 100 mm duct connection). Connect wires per the manufacturer's wiring diagram. Replace the cover and restore the circuit.

Set the humidistat trigger level: most units have a dial adjustable between 60% and 80% relative humidity. Set to 75% for a bathroom with a shower. The fan should trigger within two minutes of a shower starting and run-on until humidity drops below the setpoint.
Done well: fan has a gravity-close draught shutter, humidistat-controlled, set to 75% RH trigger. No draughts when not running; moisture removed quickly when showering. No mould on walls or ceiling.

Done badly: fan has no shutter, the duct run has a loose external grille cap that rattles in wind and stays partially open even in summer. Cold air ingress from the bathroom is a noticeable draught source. Mould at ceiling-wall junction due to incomplete post-shower extraction.
